title: Simple OT Simulation Sandbox
description: An interactive learning environment containing an attacker node (Kali), a SCADA HMI web portal (Node-RED), and a Software PLC node (OpenPLC) controlling simulated physical elements.
outcomes: Identify default ports and interfaces for industrial SCADA and PLC control services.
Exploit unauthenticated HMI services to gain RCE.
Pivot through a gateway firewall to perform Modbus network injection and OT sabotage.

levels.title: Welcome to the OT Sandbox
levels.content:
# Operational Technology (OT) Sandbox

Welcome to the Simple OT Simulation Sandbox!

This training is designed to introduce you to standard industrial protocols and visualization interfaces. You are provided with:
* An **Attacker Machine (`attacker-host`)** running Kali Linux.
* A **SCADA HMI Web Portal (`scada-hmi`)** running Node-RED.
* A **Software PLC Node (`openplc-node`)** running OpenPLC v3.

levels.title: Access the Attacker Host
levels.cloud_content:
Your first task is to access the Kali Linux GUI console.

### Access Instructions:
1. On the topology graph, select **`attacker-host`**.
2. Click the **Web Console** link to open a new tab containing the Guacamole interface.
3. Submit the passkey **`start`** to unlock the next level.
levels.local_content: Submit the passkey **`start`** to unlock the next level.

levels.title: SCADA Exploitation
levels.content:
The SCADA HMI on `10.10.10.10` runs an unauthenticated Node-RED interface on port `1880`.

Your task is to access the flow editor from Kali, construct or use a flow to run arbitrary system commands (e.g., an `exec` node), and read the first flag file located at `/root/flag.txt` on the SCADA server.

What is the flag?
levels.solution: Access the Node-RED flow editor, add an `inject` node, connect it to an `exec` node configured to run `cat /root/flag.txt`, connect that to a `debug` node, and click Deploy. Trigger the inject node to view the flag output `FLAG{SCADA_EXPLOITED_RCE}`.

hints.title: Access Flow Editor
hints.content: From Kali, you can scan `10.10.10.10` with **`nmap`** (e.g., `nmap -p 1880 10.10.10.10`) to confirm the port is open. Then, open the Kali web browser and go to `http://10.10.10.10:1880/` to open the Node-RED editor.

hints.title: Executing Commands
hints.content: In the Node-RED editor, search the left palette for the **`exec` node** (under the 'function' category). Drag it onto the canvas, configure it with the command 'cat /root/flag.txt', wire it to an `inject` node (to trigger it) and a `debug` node (to see the stdout), deploy the flow, and click the inject button.

levels.title: OT Sabotage
levels.content:
Due to firewall rules configured on the gateway router, the Kali host `attacker-host` cannot talk to the PLC (`192.168.99.10`) on Modbus port `502` or Admin port `8080`. However, the SCADA HMI host `scada-hmi` (`10.10.10.10`) is allowed to communicate with it.

Use your SCADA compromise to execute a script or network command to send a Modbus TCP write request to the OpenPLC node (`192.168.99.10:502`) setting the first **Holding Register (address 0)** to a value of **`9999`**.

Once the simulation monitoring script on the PLC detects this overload value, it will write a second flag to `/root/flag2.txt` on the PLC. Retrieve the flag content (e.g. pivoting via command execution on the HMI or using openplc commands).

What is the sabotage flag?
levels.solution: You can send Modbus commands from `scada-hmi` using python (e.g., executing a script that constructs a raw Modbus TCP TCP payload to write Holding Register 0 to `9999`). Once sent, read the flag file on the PLC by executing commands or utilizing Node-RED nodes.

hints.title: Modbus Scripting
hints.content: You can write a short **Python script** using the standard `socket` library to construct and send a raw Modbus TCP packet. Setting Register 0 to 9999 (0x270f) uses Modbus Function Code 6 (Write Single Register). Example payload for Function Code 6: \x00\x01\x00\x00\x00\x06\x01\x06\x00\x00\x27\x0f. Send this byte payload directly over a TCP socket to `192.168.99.10` on port `502`.

hints.title: Reading the PLC Flag
hints.content: Once the sabotage is triggered, the simulation daemon on the PLC writes `/root/flag2.txt`. You can use standard **`ssh`** or a python script from the HMI node to connect to the PLC and print the file's contents, or trigger a command from a Node-RED flow to fetch it.
